sanskrit_devanagari:
अन्तवन्त इमे देहा नित्यस्योक्ताः शरीरिणः ।
अनाशिनोऽप्रमेयस्य तस्माद्युध्यस्व भारत ॥ १८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
antavanta ime dehā nityasyoktāḥ śarīriṇaḥ |
anāśino 'prameyasya tasmād yudhyasva bhārata || 18 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.018 = MBh_06,024.018). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: The material body of the indestructible, immeasurable and eternal living entity is sure to come to an end; therefore, fight, O descendant of Bharata.
Only the material body is perishable; the embodied soul within is indestructible, immeasurable, and eternal. Therefore, fight, O descendent of Bharat.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt, ~290 chars of 1256-char full purport)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The material body is perishable by nature. It may perish immediately, or it may do so after a hundred years. It is a question of time only. There is no chance of maintaining it indefinitely. But the spirit soul is so minute that it cannot even be seen by an enemy, to say nothing of being killed.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Ime, these; antavantah, destructible; dehah, bodies — as the idea of reality which continues with regard to water in a mirage, etc. gets eliminated when examined with the means of knowledge, and that is its end, so are these bodies and they have an end like bodies etc. in dream and magic.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the bodies as antavanta (having an end) by an analytical examination — like water in a mirage, they dissolve under the means of knowledge (pramāṇa). The embodied (śarīriṇaḥ) is by contrast eternal (nitya), indestructible (anāśin), and immeasurable (aprameya). The 'therefore fight' (tasmād yudhyasva) is the operational consequence: since the body's perishing does not destroy what is real, action — including the warrior's action — is licensed by the doctrine just established.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The root 'dih' means 'to grow.' Hence these bodies (Dehas) are characterised by complexity. They have an end — their nature is perishablity. For, jugs and such other things which are characterised by complexity are seen to have an end. The bodies of the embodied self, which are made of conglomerate.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja derives 'deha' from the root dih (to grow) and reads the bodies as conglomerate, complex objects — like pots — that necessarily have an end. The embodied jīva is by contrast simple, eternal, immeasurable. The 'therefore fight' (tasmād yudhyasva) is the explicit licensing of action: because the body is perishable in any case and the jīva is not at all destroyed when the body is destroyed, the warrior's discharge of his prescribed duty (svadharma) is justified.

positions.rendering: since the body's perishing does not touch what is real, action is licensed
discharge your prescribed warrior duty because the body's end does not destroy the embodied

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four agree on the imperative; the bhasyas frame the licensing differently. Shankara emphasises the analytical move (the body has been seen to be antavanta, therefore the operational conclusion follows). Ramanuja emphasises the svadharma frame (Arjuna's duty as a warrior is consistent with the doctrine just established). Both readings explicitly LICENSE action — the engineering layer must not soften this into a passive 'accept change.'

positions.rendering: not within the scope of any pramāṇa (means of knowledge)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: aprameya literally means 'beyond measurement' but the technical sense in Indian philosophy is 'not falling under any pramāṇa' — perception, inference, etc. Shankara reads it epistemically (the embodied is not an object of any cognition). Ramanuja reads it ontologically (the embodied is unmeasurable because it is not composite). Translators flatten to 'immeasurable.'

literal_meaning: These bodies of the eternal, indestructible, immeasurable embodied are said to have an end. Therefore fight, O descendant of Bharata.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse closes the four-verse argument that began at 2.16. The bodies have an end (antavanta); the embodied (śarīrin) does not — it is eternal, indestructible, immeasurable. Therefore, Krishna says, fight. The 'therefore' is doctrinally important: the verse explicitly LICENSES action because what is destroyed in battle (the body) is not what is real (the embodied). Both Advaita and Vishishtadvaita commentators agree on this licensing structure; their disagreement is over whether the 'embodied' refers to the universal Self (Shankara) or the localised jīva (Ramanuja). The chapter is not endorsing fatalism; it is licensing engaged action with the understanding that the substrate of the actor is not at risk in the action.
